name: Banco Santander Chile
description: Chile's largest bank by assets and profits, focused on retail and SME banking.
businessSummary: Banco Santander Chile is the largest bank in Chile by total assets and net income. It offers retail banking, consumer lending, mortgage loans, credit cards, and wealth management services. The bank is a subsidiary of Spain's Banco Santander S.A. and is listed on the Bolsa de Comercio de Santiago and NYSE.
businessModel: Universal banking in Chile: retail loans, credit cards, mortgages, SME lending, and wealth management. Operates as a subsidiary of Spain's Banco Santander S.A.
keyRevenueDrivers: Net interest margin on the consumer and SME loan book
Fee income from transactional accounts and cards
Wealth management AUM and advisory income
SME credit demand recovery as easing cycle progresses
keyRisks: Credit quality deterioration in consumer and SME segments
NIM compression as TPM eases and spreads normalize
Regulatory capital surcharge requirements
FX funding cost volatility for USD-denominated liabilities

name: Banco de Chile
description: One of Chile's oldest and most profitable banks, with best-in-class ROE among Chilean lenders.
businessSummary: Banco de Chile is one of the country's leading financial institutions, providing retail banking, corporate finance, treasury, and insurance services. It consistently delivers best-in-class return on equity among Chilean banks. The bank is controlled by Quiñenco, the financial arm of the Luksic Group.
businessModel: Universal Chilean bank with best-in-class efficiency ratios. Segments: retail banking, wholesale/corporate, treasury operations, and brokerage through Bolchile.
keyRevenueDrivers: Net interest income on CLP/UF loan book (high structural UF exposure)
Transactional banking and card fee income
Treasury, capital markets, and FX trading income
Wholesale and SME lending book growth
keyRisks: Rising non-performing loans in the consumer segment
Spread compression in easing rate environment
Digital bank competition eroding retail deposits
Ownership concentration and related-party exposure (Luksic Group)

name: Banco de Crédito e Inversiones
description: A major Chilean bank with strong corporate and SME franchises and a US subsidiary, City National Bank.
businessSummary: BCI is one of Chile's top three banks by assets, with a strong presence in corporate, SME, and retail banking. Its US subsidiary, City National Bank of Florida, adds significant international diversification. The bank is controlled by the Yarur family and has been expanding its digital banking capabilities.

name: Inmobiliaria Las Condes
description: Chilean real estate company focused on residential and commercial development in the Las Condes district of Santiago.
businessSummary: Inmobiliaria Las Condes develops and manages residential, office, and commercial properties primarily in the upscale Las Condes municipality of Santiago. Its portfolio includes apartment buildings, office complexes, and land bank for future development.

name: Itaú Chile
description: The Chilean retail banking subsidiary of Brazil's Itaú Unibanco, one of Latin America's largest private banks.
businessSummary: Itaú Chile operates the retail and SME banking franchise of Itaú Unibanco in Chile, offering current accounts, consumer credit, mortgages, and investment products. The bank focuses on the mass-affluent and SME segments and competes directly with Santander and Banco de Chile.

name: Sociedad Química y Minera de Chile
legalName: Sociedad Química y Minera de Chile S.A.
description: World's second-largest lithium producer, also supplying iodine, specialty plant nutrition, and industrial chemicals.
businessSummary: SQM is a global leader in lithium carbonate and lithium hydroxide production, extracting brine from the Atacama Desert. It also produces iodine, specialty potassium fertilizers, and solar salts. The company's financials are highly sensitive to lithium spot prices, which collapsed in 2023-2024 after pandemic-era highs.
businessModel: Extracts lithium and iodine from brine at the Salar de Atacama under a CORFO concession. Sells battery-grade lithium to EV/battery manufacturers globally. Secondary segments: specialty plant nutrition, industrial chemicals, and solar salts.
keyRevenueDrivers: Lithium carbonate / hydroxide spot and contract prices
Lithium sales volumes to battery supply chain
Iodine prices (secondary, but high-margin)
Atacama brine extraction capacity expansion
keyRisks: Prolonged lithium price depression from Chinese oversupply and weak EV demand
CORFO contract renegotiation and royalty increases
Competition from Direct Lithium Extraction (DLE) technologies
Water use and environmental restrictions at the Atacama

name: CAP S.A.
description: Chile's only integrated steel and iron ore producer, with operations in mining, pelletizing, and steel manufacturing.
businessSummary: CAP operates through three segments: iron ore mining and pelletizing at Compañía Minera del Pacífico, steel production at Compañía Siderúrgica Huachipato, and infrastructure services. Revenues are heavily tied to global iron ore prices and Chinese steel demand, creating significant earnings volatility.

name: Enel Américas
description: Latin America's largest electricity distribution company, operating in Brazil, Argentina, Colombia, and Peru.
businessSummary: Enel Américas is a subsidiary of Italy's Enel Group and one of the largest electric utilities in Latin America. It operates electricity distribution networks reaching over 20 million customers across Brazil, Argentina, Colombia, and Peru. The company also has generation assets and is expanding in renewables.

name: Enel Chile
description: Chile's largest electricity generator and distributor, transitioning rapidly to renewable energy.
businessSummary: Enel Chile controls Enel Generación Chile (hydro and thermal generation) and Enel Distribución Chile (largest distribution network). The company is executing an aggressive renewable transition, adding solar and wind capacity while divesting thermal assets. Regulatory tariff reviews are a key earnings driver.
businessModel: Vertically integrated electricity company in Chile. Generation (hydro, thermal, solar, wind) through Enel Generación Chile and distribution to ~2 million customers through Enel Distribución Chile. Subsidiary of Italy's Enel Group.
keyRevenueDrivers: Electricity distribution tariff review cycles (regulated, indexed to CPI/UF)
Renewable generation volumes and PPA pricing
Spot electricity market prices in the SIC grid
Thermal divestiture proceeds and capital recycling
keyRisks: Hydrology risk reducing hydro generation availability
Tariff freeze or delay by the regulator (CNE)
Stranded cost risk on thermal coal assets pending divestiture
Rising UF-indexed debt service as inflation stays elevated

name: Colbún
description: A major Chilean electricity generator with hydro-dominant capacity and a growing renewables pipeline.
businessSummary: Colbún is one of Chile's three largest electricity generators, with a portfolio dominated by hydroelectric plants in the Maule and Biobío regions. The company is investing in solar and wind generation to diversify its energy mix. Earnings are sensitive to hydrology conditions and spot electricity prices.

name: Aguas Andinas
description: The main water and sanitation concessionaire for the Santiago Metropolitan Region.
businessSummary: Aguas Andinas provides drinking water and sanitation services to approximately 8 million people in the Santiago Metropolitan Region. Its revenues are regulated and tied to tariff reviews by SISS (Superintendencia de Servicios Sanitarios). The company offers stable, utility-type cash flows with high dividend payouts.

name: Empresas CMPC
description: One of Latin America's largest pulp producers, with operations in forestry, tissue, and paper across 11 countries.
businessSummary: CMPC is a diversified forest products company controlled by the Matte Group. It operates eucalyptus and pine forestry plantations across Chile, Brazil, and Argentina, pulp mills, and a leading tissue brand (Softys) across Latin America. Earnings are cyclical and tied to global pulp prices, which have been pressured since 2023.
businessModel: Integrated forest products: eucalyptus/pine plantations → bleached pulp mills → consumer tissue products (Softys, #1 brand in LatAm). Operations in 11 countries. Controlled by the Matte Group.
keyRevenueDrivers: Eucalyptus BEKP pulp spot prices (China-driven benchmark)
Softys tissue sales volumes and mix across LatAm
Forestry land bank biological asset appreciation
BRL and ARS FX movements vs. USD cost base
keyRisks: Global pulp price cycle tied to Chinese paper and packaging demand
New mill capacity additions from Arauco (Mapa) and Brazilian competitors
Softys margin compression from supermarket channel negotiation
Energy and transport cost inflation at mills

name: Empresas Copec
description: Chile's largest conglomerate by revenue, spanning fuel distribution, forestry (Arauco), and aquaculture.
businessSummary: Copec is controlled by the Angelini Group and operates across fuel distribution (COPEC brand), Arauco (a top-3 global pulp and timber producer), and Blumar (salmon and whitefish). The conglomerate has significant international diversification. Arauco's capex cycle has been a key focus for investors in recent years.
businessModel: Holding company with three pillars: (1) fuel distribution via COPEC retail network across Chile and Colombia; (2) Arauco, a global top-3 pulp, timber, and panel products company; (3) Blumar salmon and whitefish aquaculture.
keyRevenueDrivers: Arauco eucalyptus and softwood pulp prices
Arauco Mapa mill ramp-up volumes (Uruguay, operational 2022+)
COPEC fuel volume and refinery margin in Chile
Blumar salmon harvest volumes and Atlantic salmon prices
keyRisks: Arauco carrying high capex legacy debt from Mapa investment
Global pulp price downcycle reducing Arauco EBITDA
Fuel demand long-term displacement by EV transition
Salmon biological risk (SRS, sea lice) affecting Blumar output

name: Falabella
description: Latin America's largest department store operator, also running supermarkets, home improvement, and a consumer bank.
businessSummary: Falabella operates department stores, Tottus supermarkets, Sodimac home improvement stores, and Banco Falabella across Chile, Peru, Colombia, and Argentina. The company is undergoing a transformation to reduce costs and improve e-commerce profitability after significant post-pandemic margin compression. Regional FX exposure (especially Argentina) remains a key risk.
businessModel: Multi-format Latin American retailer: department stores (Falabella brand), supermarkets (Tottus), home improvement (Sodimac/HomeCentre), and consumer banking (Banco Falabella). Operates in Chile, Peru, Colombia, and Argentina.
keyRevenueDrivers: Same-store sales growth in Chile and Peru (core markets)
Banco Falabella consumer credit portfolio quality and margin
Sodimac market share and ticket size in home improvement
Tottus volume growth and fresh food margin
keyRisks: Argentina FX devaluation and hyperinflationary accounting drag
Banco Falabella non-performing loan ratio in consumer credit
E-commerce margin investment diluting EBITDA
Debt refinancing at higher rates given leverage levels

name: Cencosud
description: A leading Latin American retailer operating supermarkets, home improvement, and shopping centers across five countries.
businessSummary: Cencosud is one of Latin America's largest retail conglomerates, with supermarket banners (Jumbo, Santa Isabel, Wong), Easy home improvement stores, and Cencosud Shopping malls across Chile, Brazil, Peru, Argentina, and Colombia. The Paulmann family controls the group. Brazil's turnaround has been a recent investment focus.

name: Ripley Corp
description: A Chilean department store operator with retail banking (Banco Ripley) and mall operations in Chile and Peru.
businessSummary: Ripley Corp operates department stores in Chile and Peru, Banco Ripley (consumer credit), and a portfolio of mall assets through Mall Vivo. Consumer credit quality and retail margin recovery are the key earnings drivers. The company is smaller and less diversified than Falabella and Cencosud.

name: Parque Arauco
description: A Chilean mall developer and operator with a growing regional portfolio in Chile, Peru, and Colombia.
businessSummary: Parque Arauco develops and operates premium shopping centers in Chile, Peru, and Colombia. Its flagship assets include Parque Arauco Kennedy in Santiago and Larcomar in Lima. The company generates stable rental income indexed to inflation and benefits from the recovery in retail foot traffic post-pandemic.

name: Mall Plaza
description: Chile's largest mall operator by GLA, owned by the Falabella and Cencosud groups, with presence in Chile, Colombia, and Peru.
businessSummary: Mall Plaza is Chile's largest shopping center operator by gross leasable area, with malls across Santiago and regional cities as well as international assets. The company is controlled jointly by the Falabella and Cencosud groups. Rental income is inflation-indexed and occupancy rates have recovered strongly post-2021.

name: Entel Chile
description: Chile's second-largest telecom operator, with mobile, broadband, and enterprise IT services in Chile and Peru.
businessSummary: Entel is Chile's second-largest mobile operator and provides broadband and enterprise connectivity across Chile and Peru. The company has been investing heavily in 5G spectrum and network rollout. Peruvian operations remain a drag on group results, though management has indicated improving trends. High leverage is a watchpoint.

name: SONDA S.A.
description: Latin America's largest homegrown IT services company, providing infrastructure, software, and managed services.
businessSummary: SONDA provides IT infrastructure outsourcing, cloud services, applications management, and digital transformation consulting across 10 Latin American countries. The company has been growing inorganically through acquisitions in Brazil, Colombia, and Central America. Controlled by the Bethia Group, SONDA benefits from large public sector and banking clients.

name: Embotelladora Andina
description: A leading Coca-Cola bottler operating in Chile, Brazil, Argentina, and Paraguay.
businessSummary: Embotelladora Andina is one of Latin America's largest Coca-Cola bottlers, with franchise territories in Chile, Brazil, Argentina, and Paraguay. The company manufactures and distributes Coca-Cola products as well as local beverage brands. Regional FX exposure is a key factor for Chilean peso-denominated earnings.

name: Compañía Cervecerías Unidas
description: Chile's largest beer and beverage producer, with leading brands across wine, spirits, and soft drinks.
businessSummary: CCU is the dominant beer producer in Chile and Argentina, operating through a joint venture with Heineken. It also produces wines, spirits, and soft drinks across the region. The company is controlled by the Luksic Group (Quiñenco) and Heineken. Earnings are tied to consumer volumes and raw material cost (barley, aluminum).

name: Viña Concha y Toro
description: The world's largest exporter of South American wine, with vineyards in Chile, Argentina, and the USA.
businessSummary: Concha y Toro is Chile's most recognized wine producer and the world's top exporter of South American wines. Its portfolio spans mass-market to premium labels (Casillero del Diablo, Don Melchor, Almaviva). The company exports to over 140 countries and has subsidiaries in the UK (Fetzer) and Argentina (Trivento).

name: LATAM Airlines Group
description: Latin America's largest airline group, operating passenger and cargo services across the region and on long-haul routes.
businessSummary: LATAM Airlines Group emerged from Chapter 11 bankruptcy restructuring in 2022 and is the dominant carrier in South America, with hubs in Chile, Brazil, Colombia, Peru, and Ecuador. The airline operates a fleet of Airbus and Boeing aircraft and generates significant ancillary and cargo revenue. Post-restructuring, the balance sheet is significantly leaner.

name: Compañía Sud Americana de Vapores
legalName: Compañía Sud Americana de Vapores S.A.
description: A Chilean holding company whose main value lies in its strategic stake in Hapag-Lloyd, the German container shipping group.
businessSummary: CSAV sold most of its direct container shipping operations to Hapag-Lloyd in 2014 in exchange for a significant equity stake. Today, CSAV's primary asset is its ~17% shareholding in Hapag-Lloyd AG, traded on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange. CSAV's earnings largely mirror Hapag-Lloyd dividends and container freight market conditions.
